steps.title: Find the key signal
steps.content: The passage says that few of those farmers had actually seen Haudenosaunee farms firsthand, and it explicitly rules out independent reinvention. This sets up a transmission puzzle: if the farmers neither observed the techniques directly nor invented them on their own, the techniques must have reached them through some other route.

steps.title: Match the meaning
steps.content:
The matching choice explains the spread via intermediaries — people "more directly influenced" by Haudenosaunee practices who passed the techniques along. This is the only route the passage leaves open once direct observation and independent invention are excluded.
  • Choice A — those farmers learned the techniques from other people who were more directly influenced by Haudenosaunee practices.: fills the transmission gap left by the passage. ✓
  • Choice B — the crops typically cultivated by Euro-American farmers in the northeastern United States were not well suited to Haudenosaunee farming techniques.: shifts focus to crops, not the spread of techniques. ✗
  • Choice C — Haudenosaunee farming techniques were widely used in regions outside the northeastern United States.: addresses geography elsewhere, not how northeastern farmers obtained the techniques. ✗
  • Choice D — Euro-American farmers only began to recognize the benefits of Haudenosaunee farming techniques late in the nineteenth century.: contradicts the passage's "early nineteenth century" timeframe. ✗
